serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dry mutton curry as a side dish at a Mamak stall or Indian restaurant in Singapore is approximately 120 g, usually accompanied by rice or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Curry (Dry) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Mutton curry (dry), food code F06060012. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry mutton curry. GI estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on the negligible carbohydrate content (0.5 g/100g). The dish is predominantly protein and fat; the small carbohydrate fraction derives from spices and trace coconut milk solids. Analogous low-carbohydrate meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database consistently yield GI values below 20. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (15 × 0.5 × 1.2) / 100 = 0.09, rounded to 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 355 mg per 100 g — borderline elevated for a side-dish portion
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit dry mutton curry to a 100–120 g portion and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 120 g serving contributes approximately 426 mg sodium, representing 18–21% of the 2000 mg daily target; avoid adding further salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.5 g/100g) with high protein (19.4 g/100g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dry mutton curry is an excellent low-carbohydrate protein source for Type 2 Diabetes management; a 120 g serving contributes only 0.6 g carbohydrate and will not materially raise postprandial blood glucose, though total meal glycaemic load should be managed by limiting co-served white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~5.2 g/100g from mutton and coconut milk; sodium 355 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume dry mutton curry in moderation (no more than 120 g per occasion, no more than twice weekly) due to the combined saturated fat and sodium load; request minimal-oil preparation and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the atherogenic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit dry mutton curry to no more than once weekly, as the arachidonic acid content of red meat amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; turmeric and ginger in the preparation provide partial anti-inflammatory benefit but do not overcome the net pro-inflammatory effect of red meat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry mutton curry is a richly spiced, low-moisture preparation of mutton (sheep meat) cooked with shallots, garlic, ginger, curry powder, whole spices, and a small quantity of coconut milk until the liquid is almost entirely reduced, leaving the meat coated in an intensely flavoured, aromatic spice crust. It is a signature dish of Mamak cuisine — the Tamil Muslim culinary tradition of Singapore and Malaysia — and is commonly served at Mamak stalls, Indian Muslim restaurants, and hawker centres alongside roti canai, nasi lemak, or plain rice. The texture is firm and slightly chewy with a dry, caramelised spice coating; the flavour profile is bold, earthy, and mildly hot with warm notes of cumin, coriander, and fennel. Nutritionally, dry mutton curry is a high-protein (19.4 g/100g), moderate-fat (11.7 g/100g) food with negligible carbohydrate (0.5 g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. It is an excellent protein source for Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium is borderline elevated at 355 mg/100g, requiring portion awareness for hypertension patients. Saturated fat (~5.2 g/100g) and sodium together warrant moderation for cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is naturally halal and gluten-free. Iron content is notable at 4.1 mg/100g, supporting haematological health.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton curry dry?
Is mutton curry suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macros, secondary nutrients, and food identity sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06060012 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb/mutton data as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this is the only estimated field. GI is an analogous estimate due to negligible carbohydrate content.
notes: 1. sugar_g is set to -1: the input record carried a sentinel value of -1.0 for sugar, indicating that HPB Foodo does not report sugar separately for this entry. Sugar data for dry mutton curry is not available in the HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g (5.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (USDA NDB 17290), which is the closest approved-source analogue for cooked mutton; HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this dish. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific traditional Mamak dry mutton curry preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central composite dish entries as of 2026-04-16. 4. The input dietary_tags included 'plant_based' which is incorrect for a mutton dish and has been removed. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (low, ~15) based on negligible carbohydrate content; no direct GI measurement for dry mutton curry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
